content:
# Numbers Chapter 11 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 160–162; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0120–0121 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_011_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_011_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_011_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_011_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_011_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Fire at Taberah (vv. 1–3)

**11:1** And the people were as those who complain of evil in the ears of YHWH. And YHWH heard, and his anger was kindled, and the fire of YHWH burned among them and consumed the edge of the camp.

**11:2** And the people cried out to Moses, and Moses prayed to YHWH, and the fire sank down.

**11:3** And he called the name of that place Taberah, because the fire of YHWH burned among them.

---

## The Craving for Meat (vv. 4–9)

**11:4** And the mixed multitude that was in their midst craved a craving, and the Sons of Israel also wept again and said, "Who will feed us meat?

**11:5** We remember the fish that we ate in Egypt for nothing -- the cucumbers, and the melons, and the leeks, and the onions, and the garlic.

**11:6** And now our soul is dried up; there is nothing at all -- only toward the manna are our eyes."

**11:7** And the manna was like coriander seed, and its appearance was like the appearance of bdellium.

**11:8** The people went about and gathered it and ground it with millstones or crushed it in the mortar, and cooked it in the pot and made cakes of it; and its taste was like the taste of a cake of oil.

**11:9** And when the dew fell upon the camp at night, the manna fell upon it.

---

## Moses' Burden and Complaint (vv. 10–15)

**11:10** And Moses heard the people weeping by their families, each man at the entrance of his tent. And the anger of YHWH was kindled greatly, and in the eyes of Moses it was evil.

**11:11** And Moses said to YHWH, "Why have you done evil to your servant? And why have I not found favor in your eyes, that you lay the burden of all this people upon me?

**11:12** Did I conceive all this people? Did I give birth to them, that you should say to me, 'Carry them in your bosom, as the nursing father carries the suckling child, to the land that you swore to their fathers'?

**11:13** From where shall I get meat to give to all this people? For they weep upon me, saying, 'Give us meat that we may eat.'

**11:14** I am not able to carry all this people alone, for it is too heavy for me.

**11:15** And if thus you are doing to me, kill me, I pray -- utterly -- if I have found favor in your eyes, and let me not see my wretchedness."

---

## YHWH's Response: The Seventy Elders and the Promise of Meat (vv. 16–23)

**11:16** And YHWH said to Moses, "Gather for me seventy men from the elders of Israel, whom you know to be the elders of the people and their officers; and bring them to the Tent of Meeting, and let them station themselves there with you.

**11:17** And I will come down and speak with you there, and I will take of the spirit that is upon you and put it upon them; and they shall bear the burden of the people with you, and you shall not bear it alone."

**11:18** And to the people you shall say, 'Consecrate yourselves for tomorrow, and you shall eat meat, for you have wept in the ears of YHWH, saying, "Who will feed us meat? For it was good for us in Egypt." And YHWH will give you meat, and you shall eat.

**11:19** Not one day shall you eat, nor two days, nor five days, nor ten days, nor twenty days,

**11:20** but a whole month of days, until it comes out of your nostrils and becomes loathsome to you, because you have rejected YHWH who is in your midst, and you have wept before him, saying, "Why is it that we came out of Egypt?"'"

**11:21** And Moses said, "Six hundred thousand footmen are the people in whose midst I am, and you have said, 'I will give them meat, and they shall eat a whole month of days.'

**11:22** Shall flocks and herds be slaughtered for them, that it should suffice for them? Or shall all the fish of the sea be gathered for them, that it should suffice for them?"

**11:23** And YHWH said to Moses, "Is the hand of YHWH too short? Now you shall see whether my word will come to pass for you or not."

---

## The Spirit upon the Seventy Elders (vv. 24–30)

**11:24** And Moses went out and spoke to the people the words of YHWH. And he gathered seventy men from the elders of the people and stationed them around the Tent.

**11:25** And YHWH came down in the cloud and spoke to him, and he took of the spirit that was upon him and put it upon the seventy men, the elders. And it came to pass, when the spirit rested upon them, that they prophesied, but they did not continue.

**11:26** And two men had remained in the camp; the name of the one was Eldad, and the name of the second was Medad. And the spirit rested upon them -- and they were among those written down, but they had not gone out to the Tent -- and they prophesied in the camp.

**11:27** And a young man ran and told Moses, and said, "Eldad and Medad are prophesying in the camp."

**11:28** And Joshua the son of Nun, the attendant of Moses from his chosen ones, answered and said, "My lord Moses, restrain them!"

**11:29** And Moses said to him, "Are you jealous for my sake? Would that all the people of YHWH were prophets, that YHWH would put his spirit upon them!"

**11:30** And Moses gathered himself into the camp, he and the elders of Israel.

---

## The Quail and the Plague (vv. 31–35)

**11:31** And a wind went out from YHWH, and it drove quail from the sea and cast them upon the camp, about a day's journey on this side and about a day's journey on that side, all around the camp, and about two cubits above the face of the ground.

**11:32** And the people rose up all that day and all the night and all the next day, and gathered the quail. He who gathered least gathered ten homers. And they spread them out for themselves all around the camp.

**11:33** The meat was still between their teeth, before it was cut off, when the anger of YHWH was kindled against the people, and YHWH struck the people with an exceedingly great plague.

**11:34** And he called the name of that place Kibroth-hattaavah, because there they buried the people who had the craving.

**11:35** From Kibroth-hattaavah the people journeyed to Hazeroth, and they were at Hazeroth.

---

## Council Notes

**Manuscript observations (Leningrad Codex, pp. 160–162):** The chapter begins on page 160 immediately following the inverted-nun passage of 10:35–36. An open parashah marker (pe) appears before v. 4, creating a clear division between the Taberah incident (vv. 1–3) and the extended quail-and-elders narrative (vv. 4–35). The text extends across pages 161–162, concluding with a setumah (samekh) before 12:1. The hapax legomenon mit'onenim (v. 1) and the reduplicated hapax asafsuf (v. 4) are both clearly legible without scribal correction. Moses' maternal language in v. 12 (hariti, yelidtihu) is vocalized without ambiguity. The escalating numerals in v. 19 are written in full lexical form.

**Manuscript observations (Codex Vaticanus, pa_0120–0121):** The Vaticanus translates rather than transliterates the etiological place-names: Taberah becomes Empurismos ("Burning") in v. 3, and Kibroth-hattaavah becomes Mnemata tes Epithumias ("Graves of Desire") in v. 34, while Hazeroth in v. 35 is transliterated as Aseroth. In v. 7, the Vaticanus renders bedolakh (bdellium) as krustallos ("crystal"), a notable interpretive divergence favoring translucency over resinous identification. In v. 11, massa ("burden") is rendered as horme ("impulse/onslaught") rather than the expected bastagma or phortion ("load"), suggesting the translator understood Moses' burden as the relentless pressure of the people's demands rather than static weight. A slight spacing before v. 16 marks the transition from the people's complaint to YHWH's response.

**Structural note:** Numbers 11 contains three interlocking episodes: (1) the fire at Taberah (vv. 1–3), a compressed pattern of complaint, fire, intercession, and cessation; (2) the craving for meat and Moses' burden (vv. 4–15), which generates two distinct divine responses; and (3) YHWH's dual answer -- the spirit distributed upon seventy elders (vv. 16–17, 24–30) resolving Moses' leadership crisis, and the quail followed by plague (vv. 18–20, 31–34) answering the people's demand for meat. The parenthetical manna description (vv. 7–9) is a narrative aside addressed to the reader, countering the people's complaint with the objective reality of divine provision. The word ruakh appears in two senses: as the prophetic spirit distributed to the elders (vv. 17, 25–26, 29) and as the physical wind that drives the quail (v. 31) -- a deliberate theological wordplay in which the same divine force empowers leadership and controls nature. Moses' wish in v. 29 ("Would that all the people of YHWH were prophets") is one of the Torah's most forward-looking theological statements, later echoed by Joel 3:1 [Eng. 2:28] and cited at Pentecost (Acts 2:17–18).
